coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots and g-C3N4 composite with thermally activated delayed fluorescence for white-light illumination and anticounterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenquan
Tian, Zhen
Wang, Hui
Zhu, Xiaodong
Qian, Zixuan
Li, Hui
Bao, Xin
Yuan, Xi
coredata.dc:description: Long-lived afterglow materials have attracted considerable interest in the fields of information security and illumination. Herein, a novel carbon dot (CD)-based thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) material was synthesized by embedding sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) into a graphitic carbon nitride matrix. The formation of covalent bonds between the matrix and SCDs enhances the stabilization of the triplet state in the composite, effectively suppressing nonradiative transitions. This stabilization, coupled with the effects of sulfur doping and covalent bonding, reduces the singlet–triplet splitting energy in the composite, thereby facilitating reverse intersystem crossing and resulting in TADF emission. This composite material has been successfully employed in the development of three types of CD-based white light–emitting diodes (WLEDs) excited by blue light. These WLEDs exhibit color temperatures of 7641, 5590, and 3215 K, with CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40), respectively. The corresponding values of the color rendering index are recorded as 81.86, 90.13, and 75.07. In addition, this research provides a brief demonstration of the potential of the composite in information encryption and visible-light encryption applications. Furthermore, this study contributes to the advancement of CD utilization in the WLED field, promoting the development of next-generation CD-based WLEDs with exceptional properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sulfur-doping
Graphitic carbon nitride
Temperature activated delayed fluorescence
White light–emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of morphology, surface, and optical properties of SCDs
Afterglow luminescence mechanism of SCD@g-C3N4 composite materials
Single-component multicolor temperature delay WLED and information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of SCDs with an inset showing the image). b) Full XPS spectrum of SCDs. c) UV–vis absorption spectra of SCDs and BCDs. d) Excitation-photoluminescence maps of an aqueous solution of SCDs.

a) SEM image of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material. b) FTIR spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material. c) Full XPS spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material. High-resolution d) C 1s, e) N 1s, and f) O 1s XPS spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material.

a) Absorption spectra of SCD@g-C3N4 and BCD@g-C3N4 composite materials. b) Excitation-photoluminescence maps of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material. c) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material excited at 360 nm. d) Photos of SCD@g-C3N4 composite powder under UV light (365 nm), blue light (450 nm), and visible light, with photos showing the powder with excitation light off.

Temperature-dependent a) photoluminescence spectra and b) afterglow spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material. c) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material excited by 360 nm at 77 K. d) Afterglow decay curves of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material (measured at 426 and 528 nm) excited by 360 nm.
Proposed mechanism of the photophysical processes in SCD@g-C3N4 composite material composites excited by 360 nm and 450 nm.

Spectrum of WLEDs with color temperature of a) 3215, b) 5590, and c) 7641 K. d) CIE coordinates of WLEDs with three color temperatures at (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40). e) SCD@g-C3N4 used to display data encryption.
Schematic of the process for preparing SCDs and SCD@g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots and g-C3N4 composite with thermally activated delayed fluorescence for white-light illumination and anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Long-lived afterglow materials have attracted considerable interest in the fields of information security and illumination. Herein, a novel carbon dot (CD)-based thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) material was synthesized by embedding sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) into a graphitic carbon nitride matrix. The formation of covalent bonds between the matrix and SCDs enhances the stabilization of the triplet state in the composite, effectively suppressing nonradiative transitions. This stabilization, coupled with the effects of sulfur doping and covalent bonding, reduces the singlet–triplet splitting energy in the composite, thereby facilitating reverse intersystem crossing and resulting in TADF emission. This composite material has been successfully employed in the development of three types of CD-based white light–emitting diodes (WLEDs) excited by blue light. These WLEDs exhibit color temperatures of 7641, 5590, and 3215 K, with CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40), respectively. The corresponding values of the color rendering index are recorded as 81.86, 90.13, and 75.07. In addition, this research provides a brief demonstration of the potential of the composite in information encryption and visible-light encryption applications. Furthermore, this study contributes to the advancement of CD utilization in the WLED field, promoting the development of next-generation CD-based WLEDs with exceptional properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of long-lived afterglow photoluminescent materials has garnered increasing attention in various fields due to their ultralong lifetimes and rich excited-state features [1,2]. Carbon dots (CDs) are particularly notable for their excellent luminescent properties, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [3,4], making them widely used materials in sensing [5,6], illumination [7,8], and medical diagnostics/therapy [9–11]. Recent extensive investigations into CDs have focused on their potential in room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) materials [12–14], which are applicable in information encryption [15–17], bioimaging [18,19], and illumination [20]. The afterglow emission of CDs originates from the radiative transition of triplet states [21,22]. In particular, RTP emission arises from the radiative transition of triplet excitons to the ground state [23–25]. Additionally, excitons in triplet states can return to the singlet state through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), followed by a radiative transition that generates TADF emission. Inhibiting the quenching of the excited triplet state and reducing the energy gaps between singlet and triplet states (△EST) are two key factors for achieving high-efficiency TADF emission [26–28].

Single-component-matrix white light–emitting diodes (WLEDs) are expected to achieve efficient photoluminescence by harnessing the multilevel emission of the singlet and triplet states [29–31]. A wide bandgap is crucial for improving the color rendering index (CRI). Long-afterglow luminescent materials incorporating CDs with high quantum yields (QYs) have been extensively studied for their application in single-component, double-emission WLEDs [32–34]. For instance, Li et al. reported RTP CD-based materials with high white-light emissive efficiency (QY: 46 %) and developed a WLED with CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.38) and a CRI value of 82 [35]. Although TADF is expected to utilize excitons more effectively than RTP, RISC requires a narrow energy gap (<0.3 eV). Embedding CDs in matrix materials such as boron aluminate or zeolite can reduce the energy bandgap and promote the intersystem crossing process and TADF emission. However, CD TADF materials generally exhibit a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) due to the typically high bandgaps (>0.3 eV), which challenges the RISC process and limits effective TADF emissions, resulting in fewer reported developments of WLEDs based on CDs with TADF emissions. It is anticipated that TADF emissions could achieve higher QYs. The incorporation of carbon nanoparticles into rigid matrix materials is expected to substantially narrow the bandgap, while preserving the trilinear state and enabling TADF emission. For example, in 2023, Song et al. synthesized a novel CD material incorporated into a rigid crystalline network, exhibiting enhanced thermally delayed emission characteristics [36]. Both experimental and theoretical studies have underscored the pivotal role of the rigid crystalline network in facilitating the RISC process and suppressing nonradiative transitions, thereby enhancing TADF. However, most TADF emissions from CD materials exhibit narrow emission bands and require UV light excitation, limiting their application in the WLED field.

Herein, sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) composites were synthesized using a straightforward microwave method. The covalent bonding between the CDs and g-C3N4 forms a rigid composite structure, which protects the trilinear state, reduces the bandgap, and enables efficient TADF emission based on the CDs. The TADF emission in this composite can be excited by UV or blue light. Notably, the incorporation of sulfur modifies the bandgap of the CDs, resulting in a considerable expansion of the emission bands from 75 to >130 nm. Owing to the high quantum efficiency of fluorescence and afterglow double emission, we have successfully developed WLEDs with three distinct color temperatures using SCD@g-C3N4. The respective color temperatures are 7641, 5590, and 3215 K, with corresponding CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40). Additionally, the CRI values for these WLEDs are measured at 81.86, 90.13, and 75.07.

Urea (99 %) and m-phenylenediamine (99 %) were purchased from Macklin (China), sulfuric acid and ethanol were purchased from Guang fu. Deionized water was purified by a purification system in our laboratory.

Dissolve 0.6 g of m-phenylenediamine in 35 mL of ethanol using a beaker and stir until a transparent solution is obtained. Subsequently, add 2 mL of sulfuric acid dropwise, resulting in the formation of a white paste. Transfer this paste solution to a 50 mL autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon). Heat the autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h and then centrifuge the reacted solution at a speed of 8000 revolutions per minute for 10 min to remove any large particles. The supernatant should be filtered using a 0.22 μm filter paper. Subsequently, the filtered solution needs to be mixed with water at a ratio of 1:10. Then, the resulting mixture should be dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag filled with deionized water for 24 h to eliminate any remaining precursors and small molecules. Finally, collect the solution from the dialysis bag and subject it to freeze-drying in order to obtain SCDs powder.

The composites were prepared using a one-step microwave-assisted heating method: specifically, 5 g of urea was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water in a beaker and stirred until completely dissolved, followed by the addition of 0.05 g of SCDs powder. The resulting mixture was then heated in a 750 W microwave until complete vaporization of the water occurred, leaving behind only white honeycomb solids in the beaker. Finally, the white solid was ground to obtain a composite powder.
The synthesis of BCDs and BCD@g-C3N4 are similar to SCDs and SCD@g-C3N4, the detail processes were exhibit in Supporting Information.

The visible UV-absorption spectra of SCDs and SCD@g-C3N4 composites were obtained by Shimazu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were obtained by Perkin-Elmer spectrometer, and XPS analysis was performed by KRATOS Axis Ultra spectrometer using Al ka as excitation source. XRD data were collected on a Siemens D5005 diffractometer. TEM images were obtained by JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy, and SEM images were obtained by JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscopy. Fluorescence excitation emission pattern, fluorescence spectrum, afterglow spectrum and lifetime decay curve were measured by Horiba Jobin Yvon-3 fluorescence spectrometer and phosphorescence system. The PLQY was measured by an Otsuka QE-2000 with an integrating sphere. The emission spectrum of LED was obtained by using labsphere spectrometer.

Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves were collected under 360 nm excitation, and average luminescence lifetimes were calculated using the following equation (1):
(1)
τ
a v g
=
∑
a i
t i 2
∑
a i
t i
Time-resolved luminescence decay curves were fitted with tri-exponential functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of morphology, surface, and optical properties of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solvothermal method was employed to synthesize SCDs, and their morphology was first investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the TEM images show a uniform distribution of particles with an average size of approximately 2 nm (Fig. S1). The inset of the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image reveals lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. The surface structure of SCDs was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD spectrum of SCDs exhibits a diffraction peak at 22.9°, indicative of amorphous carbon. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3) displays a broad absorption band ranging from 2700 to 3700 cm−1, arising from the stretching vibrations of O–H, C–H, and N–H bonds [37]. The peaks at 1710, 1603, and 1010 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=O, C=N, and C–S bonds, respectively [37,38]. The full XPS spectrum of SCDs (Fig. 1b) shows carbon content at 68.15 %, with nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur at 1.52 %, 27.5 %, and 2.82 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS C1s spectra (Fig. S4a) display three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C=C/C–C, C–N/C–S, and C=O, respectively [39,40]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S4b) shows two peaks at 399.7 and 400.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic and pyrrolic N [41]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S4c) features two peaks at 531.3 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–O groups [39]. The S2p spectrum (Fig. S4d) reveals binding energies of 163.4 eV for S2p 3/2 and 164.8 eV for S2p 1/2 [41]. These FTIR and XPS results confirm the successful incorporation of sulfur into SCDs.

The photophysical properties of SCDs were further explored through absorption spectra (Fig. 1c) and fluorescence excitation-emission spectra (Fig. 1d). Compared to BCDs, which contain no sulfur, SCDs exhibit a broader absorption peak ranging from 350 to 520 nm. The photoluminescence excitation-emission spectrum reveals a luminescence center at 520 nm with an optimal excitation wavelength of 460 nm (Fig. 1d). The photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) at 365 nm is 54.61 %, and the fluorescence lifetime is 6.2 ns (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the PLQY of SCDs was also measured as 63.17 % at 460 nm (Fig. S6). In comparison to the excitation-emission spectrum of BCDs (Fig. S7), there is a noticeable redshift after the incorporation of sulfur. These results demonstrate that the incorporation of sulfur modifies the bandgap of CDs, leading to a redshift in the emission peak. Notably, aggregation-induced quenching and the presence of dissolved oxygen in water quench the CDs triplet, resulting in no afterglow emission of SCDs in the powder and aqueous states (Fig. S8).

We synthesized SCD@g-C3N4 composites using a straightforward microwave-assisted method by mixing SCDs with a urea aqueous solution. The morphology and structure of SCD@g-C3N4 were examined. The TEM images of SCD@g-C3N4 reveal the SCDs embedded in matrix materials (Fig. S9). The SEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that the composite exhibits a stacked configuration of delicate nanosheets. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b) display absorption peaks at 1495 and 1710 cm−1, attributed to the tensile vibrations of C–N and C=O, respectively, while the range of 2500–3600 cm−1 shows the tensile vibrations of O–H, N–H, and C–H bonds [37]. The full XPS spectrum of the composite (Fig. 2c) reveals elemental concentrations of C, N, O, and S at 47.23 %, 28.17 %, 24.15 %, and 0.44 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2d) deconvolutes into three peaks at binding energies of 284.7, 285.7, and 289.3 eV, corresponding to C=C, C–N/C=N, and C=O groups, respectively [37]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) exhibits two peaks at 399.7 and 400.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively [41]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) contains bands at 531.3 and 532.2 eV, assigned to C=O and C–O groups [40]. The HR-XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. S10) shows peaks at 162.5 and 163.6 eV, corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively [39]. The FTIR and XPS spectra confirm that SCDs are covalently embedded in the matrix material. To explore the effect of microwave heating time on the matrix material of the composite, XRD spectra of samples synthesized at different times (165, 195, and 215 s) were measured (Fig. S11). With increasing microwave time, the intensity of the (022) diffraction peak at 29.67° for g-C3N4 gradually enhanced. Concurrently, SEM images of these samples (Fig. S12) after different microwave heating durations showed that the morphologies evolved from nanoparticle to nanoparticle. This transition makes the luminescence properties of the composite materials deteriorated after long time heating (Fig. S13).

The photoluminescent properties of the composites were further investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectra of SCD@g-C3N4 and BCD@g-C3N4 composite powders (Fig. 3 a) show that the SCD@g-C3N4 composite exhibits two additional broad absorption peaks at 365 and 480 nm. The excitation-emission spectrum of SCD@g-C3N4 composite powder shown in Fig. 3b features a broader emission band with a blue emission center peaking at 419 nm and a green emission center at 523 nm. Compared to the BCD@g-C3N4 composite, the SCD@g-C3N4 composite generates a distinct green emission center (Fig. S14). The fluorescence lifetimes at 419 and 523 nm under 365 nm excitation are 7.2 and 7.1 ns, respectively (Fig. S15). Notably, the FWHM extends to 130 nm, and the PLQY of SCD@g-C3N4 was measured at 46.86 % (Fig. S16). These properties demonstrate the material's notable potential in photoinduced LED applications. The SCD@g-C3N4 composite also exhibits outstanding afterglow emission. As shown in Fig. 3c, the composite displays two afterglow emission peaks centered at 426 and 528 nm, which align well with the photoluminescence spectrum. Based on the photoluminescence spectra, the afterglow in SCD@g-C3N4 composite is attributed to TADF. As depicted in Fig. 3d, under UV light excitation at 365 nm, the composite powders exhibit vivid cyan fluorescence, and when the UV light is turned off, a green afterglow signal visible to the naked eye persists for up to 7 s. Remarkably, the green afterglow emission can also be observed for up to 1 s after excitation with visible light (450 nm or white-light flashlight).

To further investigate the luminescence mechanism of the composite material, temperature-dependent photoluminescent spectra of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, two peaks of the fluorescence spectra remain unchanged in position and intensity as the temperature increases from 80 to 300 K. The afterglow spectra of the composite at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 4b. With the increase in temperature, the peaks at 448 and 518 nm gradually disappear; additionally, two peaks appear at 426 and 528 nm, and their intensity increases, indicating that these afterglow peaks are due to TADF emission. The afterglow intensity obviously increased with the temperature, and the maximum TADF signal could be measured at 300 K. The afterglow spectrum measured at 77 K can be fitted to three peaks at 448, 488, and 523 nm (Fig. S17a). By comparing the afterglow spectra of g-C3N4 and SCD@g-C3N4 at 77 K (Fig. S17b), the peaks at 448 and 523 nm can be attributed to the SCD@g-C3N4 composite. The singlet–triplet energy gaps (ΔEst) of the SCD@g-C3N4 composites were calculated from the fluorescence and afterglow spectra measured at 77 K. The ΔEst values for the two energy bands in the composites were calculated to be 0.21 and 0.014 eV (the detailed calculation process is in the support information). These small ΔEst values could greatly promote the RISC process, further verifying that the afterglow in the composite is due to TADF. Matrix recombination plays a crucial role in stabilizing the triplet state of SCDs, limiting the nonradiative transition, reducing ΔEst, and promoting the crossing from triplet to singlet states in radiating systems, thus enabling TADF emission. The TADF decay curve of the composite was fitted with a triple exponential function (as shown in Fig. 4d), and the average lifetimes of the composite at 426 and 528 nm were calculated to be 659 and 687 ms, respectively, when excited by 360 nm. Time-resolved luminescence decay curves were fitted with tri-exponential functions, the detail analysis were shown in Fig. S18.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Afterglow luminescence mechanism of SCD@g-C3N4 composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results presented above, we propose that the addition of the sulfur element alters the bandgap of the CDs, resulting in a redshift of the excitation/emission wavelengths and forming a new green emission center. The composite matrix substantially enhances the luminescence performance of the CDs. From our previous work, it is evident that lamellar g-C3N4 substrates are particularly conducive to long-afterglow emission [37,42]. The covalent bond formed between the substrate and the CDs effectively prevents CD aggregation, facilitating solid-state luminescence. Additionally, the synergy between the CDs and the matrix stabilizes the CDs’ triplet states, inhibits nonradiative transitions, and reduces the energy gap (ΔEst) between the singlet and triplet states to <0.3 eV. This small ΔEst ensures efficient RISC, leading to the exhibition of TADF emission by the composite. The luminescence mechanism of the composites is depicted in Fig. 5 . Low temperatures inhibit the nonradiative transitions and the RISC process in composites, resulting in the predominance of RTP emission (exciton pathways: S1–T1–S0 and S2–T2–S0) during afterglow. An increase in temperature facilitates nonradiative transitions and the RISC process, leading to a decrease in RTP strength. Conversely, TADF emission intensifies with enhanced RISC, and the composite materials predominantly exhibit TADF emission (exciton pathways: S1–T1–S1–S0 and S2–T2–S2–S0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-component multicolor temperature delay WLED and information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its broader emission bands and excellent photoluminescent properties under blue light excitation, the SCD@g-C3N4 composite was employed as a white phosphor in the field of illumination. However, it is difficult to prepare WLED with high CRI based on SCDs@g-C3N4 composite using excitation source of 365 nm InGaN microchip because of the cyan (dual emission of blue and green light) photoluminescence emission (Fig. S19). Therefore, we used the SCD@g-C3N4 composite material as a color conversion layer in delay-type WLEDs. The surface of the indium gallium nitride (InGaN) microchip, which emits light at 450 nm, was coated with a composite material blended with epoxy resin in specific proportions. This process led to the successful fabrication of a three-color-temperature WLED (Fig. 6 a, b and 6c), because of the inevitable consecutive reabsorption [43]. When activated the three WLEDs exhibit high-efficiency white-light emission across a wide spectral range of 400–700 nm. The corresponding CIE coordinates are (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40), and the respective color temperatures are 7641, 5590, and 3215 K (Fig. 6d). Additionally, the CRI values for these emissions are recorded as 81.86, 90.13, and 75.07. Due to continuous reabsorption, the WLED spectrum exhibits a greater redshift compared to the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the SCD@g-C3N4 composite. For example, a WLED with a color temperature of 5590 K can sustain its afterglow emission for >2 s after being switched off (Fig. S20), providing a buffer time to reduce transient blindness in human eyes. Furthermore, we demonstrated a straightforward anti-counterfeiting technique using SCD@g-C3N4 based on the properties of TADF visible-light excited. As shown in Fig. 6e, the larger star in the security patterns was written by SCD@g-C3N4 composite, and the smaller pattern was created using commercial phosphor powders. Upon turning off the 365 nm light, an obvious afterglow signal of the larger star could be observed by eye. And after a white light flashlight in mobile phone irradiated, the similar security patterns with green afterglow signal also could be identified. Based on these phenomena the simple concept of anti-counterfeiting was realized, which demonstrating the great potential of SCD@ g-C3N4 composite in advanced security application.

In summary, SCD@g-C3N4 composite was synthesized through a microwave-assisted method by embedding SCDs in g-C3N4 matrix materials. This composite exhibits high-efficiency photoluminescence with emission peaks at 419 and 523 nm (PLQY: 46.86 %) and a broad FWHM exceeding 130 nm. Additionally, it demonstrates TADF emission peaks at 426 and 528 nm, with lifetimes of 659 and 687 ms, respectively. Further investigations indicate that the introduction of sulfur element doping and the interaction between the matrix and CDs effectively suppress nonradiative transitions, reduce ΔEst, and facilitate RISC processes, thereby generating TADF emission. Owing to its high quantum yield in the visible region, wide FWHM, and sensitivity to blue light excitation, this composite material was used as the color conversion layer for coating on a 450 nm luminous LED chip. Consequently, we successfully developed a single-component delayed WLED with three color temperatures. The resulting CIE coordinates are (0.30, 0.29), (0.33, 0.30), and (0.42, 0.40), corresponding to color temperatures of 7641, 5590, and 3215 K, respectively, while achieving CRI values of approximately 81.86, 90.13, and 75.07. The potential application of this composite material in information encryption was also briefly demonstrated.

Wenquan Sun: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Zhen Tian: Methodology. Hui Wang: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Xiaodong Zhu: Data curation. Zixuan Qian: Data curation. Hui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xin Bao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xi Yuan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (YDZJ202301ZYTS254, 20230508058RC).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115914.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot-based nanocomposite: long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence for lifetime thermal sensing
Dyes Pigm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Liu, X. Kang, H.Y. Zhang, Y.C. Liu, C.Y. Wang, X.R. Gao, Y.R. Li, Carbon dot-based nanocomposite: Long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence for lifetime thermal sensing, Dyes Pigm. 181 (2020) 108576.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boric acid-activated room-temperature phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence for efficient solid-state photoluminescence materials
Adv. Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.H. Wang, Z.D. Lu, X.M. Qin, Z. Zhang, Y.E. Shi, J.W.Y. Lam, Z.G. Wang, B.Z.
